# MCP Server Design

Design MCP servers as a **stable contract** between an AI host (here: LangGraph agents running in GitHub Actions, plus the Next.js dashboard) and your tools/data. Get the primitive choice and the schemas right first; the SDK wiring is the easy part. Prefer linking the official docs below over memorizing version-pinned APIs — the spec and SDKs move fast (latest spec `2025-11-25`, RC `2026-07-28`; FastMCP 3.0 shipped Jan 2026).

## Pull current docs first

Always re-fetch before designing — do not trust training memory for API shape:

- Architecture (layers, lifecycle, primitives): https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/learn/architecture
- Server concepts (tools/resources/prompts, who controls what): https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/learn/server-concepts
- Tools (schemas, structured content, output schema, errors): https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/tools
- Transports (stdio, Streamable HTTP, sessions, security): https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/transports
- Build a server (SDK quickstarts): https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/develop/build-server
- Latest spec index: https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/latest
- TS SDK: https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/typescript-sdk · Python SDK: https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/python-sdk · FastMCP: https://gofastmcp.com

For exact current API signatures, prefer Context7 or the SDK `docs/` on GitHub over this file.

## Pick the right primitive (this is the design decision)

MCP exposes three server primitives, distinguished by **who controls invocation**:

- **Tools** — *model-controlled* actions with side effects or computed answers (query Aurora, run an extractor, kick a Playwright capture). Each is one operation with typed input (and ideally output) schema. The LLM decides when to call.
- **Resources** — *application-controlled* read-only context addressed by URI (`evidence://relrun_001/git_diff`, a feature manifest, a `SKILL.md` snapshot). Use direct URIs for fixed data, **resource templates** (`evidence://{release_run_id}/{type}`) for parameterized reads. The app, not the model, chooses what to load.
- **Prompts** — *user-controlled* templates surfaced as slash commands / buttons (e.g. a "draft release blog" template with arguments). Explicit invocation only.

Rule of thumb: if it changes state or computes, it's a **tool**; if it's read-only context the app injects, it's a **resource**; if it's a reusable human-triggered workflow, it's a **prompt**. Don't model read-only evidence retrieval as a tool just because tools are easier to call from LangGraph — but see Gotchas, because most LangGraph clients only consume tools.

## Tool schema design

- Give every tool a unique, namespaced `name` (`evidence_extract_ui_strings`, not `extract`), a human `title`, and a description that says **when** to use it, not just what it does — the description is the model's only routing signal.
- Define a JSON Schema `inputSchema`: types, `required`, `enum`s, `description` per field, `default`s. This is your validation boundary — validate again server-side regardless.
- Provide an `outputSchema` for anything structured. When present, the server MUST return conforming `structuredContent` AND (for back-com&shy;comp) a serialized-JSON text block. This lets LangGraph nodes consume typed objects instead of re-parsing prose.
- Two error channels: **protocol errors** (JSON-RPC `error`: unknown tool, bad args) vs **tool execution errors** (`result` with `isError: true`: API failure, business-logic rejection). A guardrail rejection or unsupported-claim failure is a tool execution error the model can react to — not a protocol crash.
- Use `annotations` (readOnlyHint, destructiveHint, idempotentHint) to hint behavior, but treat annotations from untrusted servers as advisory only.
- Return resource links / embedded resources from tools to hand back large artifacts (S3 URIs, screenshots) instead of inlining megabytes.

## Transport

- **stdio** — local subprocess, one client, newline-delimited JSON-RPC. Default for a server the GitHub Actions runner launches in-process. Never write non-MCP bytes to stdout (logs go to stderr).
- **Streamable HTTP** — single endpoint (POST + optional GET/SSE), many clients, sessions via `Mcp-Session-Id`. Use for a remote/shared server (e.g. one the Vercel dashboard or multiple runners hit). Replaces the deprecated HTTP+SSE transport.
- HTTP security is mandatory, not optional: validate the `Origin` header (DNS-rebinding defense), bind localhost-only when local, require auth (OAuth/bearer), send `MCP-Protocol-Version` header. See Gotchas.

## Wiring into this stack (LangGraph orchestration)

- **Tool wiring**: adapt MCP tools into LangGraph tool nodes (e.g. via `langchain-mcp-adapters` / an MCP client session). Keep MCP tools thin and deterministic; let the **graph** own control flow, retries, and routing — don't bury branching logic inside a tool.
- **State/handoff**: MCP is stateless per call; persistence lives in LangGraph state + Aurora. Pass `release_run_id` / `thread_id` as explicit tool args so any node (or a resumed run) can re-fetch evidence — never rely on server-side memory between calls.
- **Approval gates**: the three human gates are LangGraph `interrupt`s, not MCP features. The MCP server just produces the reviewable payload (feature manifest, artifacts, skill diff); the graph interrupts and the dashboard resumes the same `thread_id`.
- **Eval gates**: model deterministic policy checks + Bedrock Guardrails + claim→evidence linking as their own tools/nodes that gate progress, returning `isError`/structured verdicts the graph routes on. Keep eval logic out of generation tools so you can run it independently.
- **Cost/latency**: design coarse-grained tools (one round-trip per logical step) over chatty ones — every tool call is an LLM turn. Fan out independent extractors in parallel graph branches, not sequential tool calls. Cache evidence reads as resources. Push heavy/streamed output (media, large diffs) to S3 + resource links rather than through the model context. Pin cheaper Bedrock models for extraction/clustering, reserve the strong model for generation.

## Checklist

- [ ] Re-fetched the docs above; confirmed current spec version & SDK API (didn't rely on memory).
- [ ] Each capability mapped to the correct primitive (tool / resource / prompt) by controller, not convenience.
- [ ] Every tool has namespaced name, when-to-use description, `inputSchema` with required/enum/defaults, and `outputSchema` for structured returns.
- [ ] Server-side validation independent of the declared schema; inputs sanitized.
- [ ] Errors split correctly: protocol errors vs `isError: true` execution errors (guardrail/eval failures are the latter).
- [ ] Transport chosen deliberately; if HTTP: Origin validation, auth, localhost binding, protocol-version header.
- [ ] `release_run_id` / `thread_id` passed explicitly; no reliance on server session memory across calls.
- [ ] LangGraph owns control flow, retries, interrupts; MCP tools stay thin and deterministic.
- [ ] Eval/guardrail/claim-linking are gating nodes the graph routes on.
- [ ] Large artifacts returned as resource links / S3 URIs, not inlined.
- [ ] Tools are coarse-grained; independent work parallelized; model tier matched to task cost.

## Gotchas

- **Most LangGraph/LangChain MCP clients only consume tools** — `resources` and `prompts` are frequently ignored by the adapter layer. If your agent needs evidence as context, you often must expose it as a `read_*` tool too, even though semantically it's a resource. Verify what your adapter version actually surfaces before designing around resources.
- **stdio servers that print to stdout corrupt the stream.** A stray `print()` / `console.log()` / library banner on stdout is parsed as a malformed JSON-RPC message and the session dies silently. Route ALL logging to stderr; in Python this bites FastMCP servers via dependencies that log to stdout.
- **`outputSchema` is a contract you must honor both ways.** Declaring it obligates the server to return `structuredContent` *and* a serialized-JSON text block; emitting only one (or returning a shape that doesn't validate) makes strict clients reject the result. Easy to half-implement.
- **Skipping Origin validation on Streamable HTTP is a real RCE/exfil vector**, not a lint nit — a browser on the user's machine can reach a localhost MCP server via DNS rebinding and drive its tools. Validate `Origin`, bind 127.0.0.1, require auth even locally.
- **Tool descriptions are routing, not docs.** A vague description makes the LLM pick the wrong extractor or skip the eval gate. Spend words on *when to use / when not to* — this matters more than the field descriptions.
- **Chatty tool design silently 10×'s cost and latency** in a multi-graph pipeline: every call is an LLM turn billed through Bedrock. Collapse N small reads into one batched tool; parallelize independent extractors as graph branches instead of sequential calls.
- **MCP has no memory between calls; LangGraph state ≠ MCP server state.** A resumed run after an approval interrupt starts a fresh MCP session — anything you stashed in server-process memory is gone. Persist to Aurora/state and pass IDs as args.
- **Bedrock Guardrails / Converse tool-use schemas are not 1:1 with MCP `inputSchema`.** Converse has its own toolSpec shape and stricter JSON-Schema subset; don't assume an MCP schema round-trips unchanged. Translate and test (deep nesting, `oneOf`, formats often differ).
- **`tools/list_changed` only fires if you declared `listChanged: true`** at init. Dynamically (de)registering tools without declaring the capability leaves clients with a stale registry and no refresh.
- **Two FastMCP lineages exist** — the `mcp` official Python SDK's bundled FastMCP vs the standalone `fastmcp` (3.0) package — with diverging APIs. Pin and import deliberately; copy-pasted snippets often mix them.
- _none observed yet for the Playwright/ffmpeg media path specifically — capture runtime issues here as they surface._
